data.case_title: Heirs of Malabanan v. Republic of the Philippines
data.doctrine: Under Section 14(1) of PD 1529, persons who have been in open, continuous, exclusive and notorious possession and occupation of alienable and disposable lands of the public domain under a bona fide claim of ownership since June 12, 1945, or earlier, may file a petition for judicial confirmation of title. The land must be alienable and disposable at the time of the application.
data.facts: Mario Malabanan applied for original registration of a parcel of land in Silang, Cavite. He claimed open, continuous, exclusive and notorious possession since June 12, 1945. The Republic opposed on grounds that the land is part of the public domain.
data.issue: Whether open, continuous, exclusive, and notorious possession since June 12, 1945 of alienable and disposable land is sufficient for judicial confirmation of title.
data.ruling: The Supreme Court ruled that for an application under Section 14(1) of PD 1529, the land must be alienable and disposable at the time of filing. However, the disposability must be proved to have existed as of June 12, 1945 or earlier. The case was remanded for further proceedings.

data.case_title: People of the Philippines v. Cachuela
data.doctrine: In a prosecution for murder, treachery is present when the offender employs means, methods, or forms in the execution of the crime which tend directly and specially to insure its execution, without risk to himself arising from the defense which the offended party might make.
data.facts: Accuseds Ronald and Raymund Cachuela were charged with murder for the stabbing death of the victim. The Information alleged the qualifying circumstance of treachery. Evidence showed that the victim was attacked from behind without warning.
data.issue: Whether the killing was attended by the qualifying circumstance of treachery, thus qualifying the crime to murder.
data.ruling: The Supreme Court affirmed the conviction for murder. Treachery was proven because the victim was attacked from behind, giving him no opportunity to defend himself. The attack was sudden and unexpected, ensuring execution without risk to the accused.

data.case_title: Agabon v. National Labor Relations Commission
data.doctrine: Where the dismissal is for a just cause, the lack of statutory due process should not nullify the dismissal, or render it illegal, or ineffectual. However, the employer should indemnify the employee for the violation of his statutory rights in the form of nominal damages.
data.facts: Virgilio and Jenny Agabon were dismissed from Riviera Home Improvements for abandonment of work. They filed an illegal dismissal case. The employer had just cause but failed to comply with the two-notice rule.
data.issue: What is the proper legal consequence when an employer has just cause for dismissal but fails to observe the procedural due process requirements (twin-notice rule)?
data.ruling: The Supreme Court held that where there is just cause for dismissal but the employer fails to comply with the twin-notice rule, the dismissal is not ineffectual or illegal. The employer must pay nominal damages of P30,000.00 for violation of the employee's right to statutory due process.
data.tags: illegal dismissal,just cause,due process,two-notice rule,nominal damages,Agabon doctrine,labor law

data.doctrine: A contract of sale is perfected at the moment there is a meeting of minds upon the thing which is the object of the contract and upon the price. The perfection of the contract of sale does not depend on the execution of a formal document.
data.facts: Petitioner entered into a verbal agreement to purchase a parcel of land from respondent. Petitioner paid earnest money but respondent later refused to proceed with the sale. Petitioner sought specific performance.
data.issue: Whether a perfected contract of sale existed between the parties despite the absence of a written contract.
data.ruling: The Supreme Court held that a contract of sale was perfected upon the meeting of minds on the object and the price. The payment of earnest money was evidence of a perfected contract. Respondent was ordered to execute a deed of absolute sale.
data.tags: contract of sale,perfection,meeting of minds,earnest money,specific performance,verbal agreement

data.case_title: Manila Hotel Corporation v. National Labor Relations Commission
data.doctrine: The right to security of tenure is not absolute. An employer may terminate employment for just causes enumerated under the Labor Code, provided that the employee is accorded due process through the twin requirements of notice and hearing.
data.facts: Marcelo Santos, an employee of Manila Hotel, was dismissed for cause after an investigation. He filed a complaint for illegal dismissal before the NLRC alleging that the dismissal was without just cause and without due process.
data.issue: Whether the termination of the employee was valid for having just cause and having followed the procedural due process requirements.
data.ruling: The Supreme Court ruled that the employer must comply with both substantive and procedural requirements of due process. Failure to follow the two-notice rule renders the termination procedurally infirm, entitling the employee to indemnification even if there is just cause.
data.tags: security of tenure,just cause,due process,two-notice rule,dismissal,Manila Hotel,labor standards

data.name: Civil Law
data.description: Private rights, obligations, contracts, property, and torts

data.name: Criminal Law
data.description: Crimes, penalties, and offenses under the Revised Penal Code

data.name: Labor Law
data.description: Employment, workers rights, NLRC, and labor standards

data.name: Family Law
data.description: Marriage, family relations, adoption, and support

data.name: Remedial Law
data.description: Rules of procedure, evidence, and court processes

data.name: Tax Law
data.description: National Internal Revenue Code and tax jurisprudence

Where an employer dismisses an employee for a just or authorized cause but fails to comply with the procedural due process requirements (twin-notice rule), the dismissal is not rendered illegal or void. Instead, the employer is liable to pay nominal damages to the employee. The current amount of nominal damages is P30,000.00 for violation of due process in cases of just cause, and P50,000.00 for authorized causes.
data.legal_basis: Article 294, Labor Code of the Philippines; Article 2221, Civil Code of the Philippines
data.tags: Agabon,just cause,procedural due process,twin-notice rule,nominal damages,dismissal,illegal dismissal

data.description: A principal is bound by the acts of its agent when the principal has cloaked the agent with apparent authority to act on its behalf, even if no actual authority was granted. Third parties who relied in good faith on such apparent authority are protected.
data.legal_basis: Article 1910, Civil Code of the Philippines (Obligations and Contracts)

data.description: Treachery exists when the offender employs means, methods, or forms in the execution of the offense which tend directly and specially to ensure the execution without risk to himself arising from any defense the victim might make. Two elements must concur: (1) employment of means which gives the victim no opportunity to defend himself; and (2) deliberate or conscious adoption of such means.
data.legal_basis: Article 14(16), Revised Penal Code of the Philippines
data.tags: treachery,alevosia,murder,qualifying circumstance,means of attack,no opportunity to defend

data.doctrine_name: Doctrine of Exhaustion of Administrative Remedies
data.description: Before a party may seek judicial intervention, he must first exhaust all administrative remedies available to him. Failure to exhaust administrative remedies divests the courts of jurisdiction. This doctrine promotes comity between the courts and administrative agencies and gives administrative agencies the opportunity to correct their own errors.
data.legal_basis: Administrative Code of 1987; Rule 65, Rules of Court

data.description: Courts will not interfere with the business decisions of corporate officers and directors when such decisions are made honestly, in good faith, and within the bounds of authority. Corporate directors are presumed to act in the best interests of the corporation. Courts will not second-guess business decisions absent clear evidence of fraud, bad faith, or gross negligence.
data.legal_basis: Section 23, Revised Corporation Code (Republic Act No. 11232)
data.tags: business judgment rule,corporations,directors,board,good faith,fiduciary duty,corporate governance

data.title: Definition of Obligation
data.content: An obligation is a juridical necessity to give, to do, or not to do.
data.source: Civil Code of the Philippines (Republic Act No. 386)

data.title: Sources of Obligations
data.content: Obligations arise from: (1) Law; (2) Contracts; (3) Quasi-contracts; (4) Acts or omissions punished by law; and (5) Quasi-delicts.
data.source: Civil Code of the Philippines (Republic Act No. 386)

data.title: Essential Requisites of Contracts
data.content: There is no contract unless the following requisites concur: (1) Consent of the contracting parties; (2) Object certain which is the subject matter of the contract; (3) Cause of the obligation which is established.
data.source: Civil Code of the Philippines (Republic Act No. 386)

data.title: Void Contracts
data.content: The following contracts are inexistent and void from the beginning: (1) Those whose cause, object or purpose is contrary to law, morals, good customs, public order or public policy; (2) Those which are absolutely simulated or fictitious; (3) Those whose cause or object did not exist at the time of the transaction; (4) Those whose object is outside the commerce of men; (5) Those which contemplate an impossible service; (6) Those where the intention of the parties relative to the principal object of the contract cannot be ascertained; (7) Those expressly prohibited or declared void by law.
data.source: Civil Code of the Philippines (Republic Act No. 386)

data.title: Negligence — Standard of Care
data.content: The fault or negligence of the obligor consists in the omission of that diligence which is required by the nature of the obligation and corresponds with the circumstances of the persons, of the time and of the place. When negligence shows bad faith, the provisions of articles 1171 and 2201, paragraph 2, shall apply. If the law or contract does not state the diligence which is to be observed in the performance, that which is expected of a good father of a family shall be required.
data.source: Civil Code of the Philippines (Republic Act No. 386)
data.tags: negligence,diligence,good father of family,fault,standard of care

data.title: Quasi-Delict
data.content: Whoever by act or omission causes damage to another, there being fault or negligence, is obliged to pay for the damage done. Such fault or negligence, if there is no pre-existing contractual relation between the parties, is called a quasi-delict and is governed by the provisions of this Chapter.
data.source: Civil Code of the Philippines (Republic Act No. 386)

data.title: Rights of Employees
data.content: It shall be the duty of every employer to provide its workers with all the rights and benefits provided for in this Code, as well as those stipulated in the employment contract, company policy, or collective bargaining agreement, which are more favorable to the employees.
data.source: Labor Code of the Philippines (Presidential Decree No. 442)

data.title: Security of Tenure
data.content: In cases of regular employment, the employer shall not terminate the services of an employee except for a just cause or when authorized by this Title. An employee who is unjustly dismissed from work shall be entitled to reinstatement without loss of seniority rights and other privileges and to his full backwages, inclusive of allowances, and to his other benefits or their monetary equivalent computed from the time his compensation was withheld from him up to the time of his actual reinstatement.
data.source: Labor Code of the Philippines (Presidential Decree No. 442)
